perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees to accept the CONFIDENTI AL INFORMATION and to employ all reasonable efforts to maintain the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION as secret and Page 2 of 3 confidential, such efforts to be no less than the degree of care employed by RECIPIENT to preserve and safeguard RECIPIENT's own confidential informa tion.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees to accept the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION and to generally maintain the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION as confidential, using efforts it deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'all reasonable efforts' with 'generally maintain' and 'efforts it deems appropriate,' making the required level of care subjective and less stringent. This creates ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of a trade secret. The modification allows the recipient to use efforts they deem appropriate, potentially falling short of 'reasonable efforts' and thus violating the Act if the information qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1203 (2022) - Right to Damages :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1203 - Right to Damages Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1203 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. (b) Damages under this subtitle may include: (1) The actual loss caused by misappropriation; and (2) The unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. (c) In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (d) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a) of this section. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Maryland may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'all reasonable efforts' to maintain confidentiality, aligning with the standard expected to protect trade secrets. The modified text replaces this with 'generally maintain' and 'efforts it deems appropriate,' which introduces a subjective standard that may fall short of 'reasonable efforts.' The Law Explanation clarifies that this change could violate the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 2, MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1203, discusses damages for misappropriation, implying a legal standard of protection exists, which the modified text potentially undermines by weakening the required effort. This represents a clear contradiction, as the modified text allows for a lower standard of care than what's legally expected for trade secret protection, increasing the risk of misappropriation without legal recourse.

perturbation.original_text: However, RECIPIENT agrees that it will not use the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION for any purpose other than the PURPOSE without the prior written consent of PROVIDER.
perturbation.changed_text: However, RECIPIENT agrees that it will endeavor not to use the CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION for purposes significantly deviating from the PURPOSE without the PROVIDER's consent, where feasible.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will not use' to 'will endeavor not to use' weakens the prohibition. Adding 'significantly deviating' introduces subjective judgment. 'Where feasible' provides an easy out. This weakens the original confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines misappropriation as using a trade secret without consent. The modified language allows for potential uses outside the defined purpose if they aren't 'significantly deviating' or if obtaining consent is not 'feasible,' creating a loophole not permitted by the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1202 (2022) - Misappropriation of Trade Secrets May Be Enjoined :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1202 - Misappropriation of Trade Secrets May Be Enjoined Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1202 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. (b) Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (c) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. (d) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (e) In this section, “exceptional circumstances” includes a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the confidentiality agreement, creating a potential loophole. The original text states RECIPIENT 'will not use' confidential information, while the changed text states they 'will endeavor not to use' it for purposes 'significantly deviating' from the PURPOSE, 'where feasible.' This introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. The Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1202 allows for injunctions against misappropriation of trade secrets. The modification introduces exceptions (significantly deviating, where feasible) that are not contemplated by the law. The law does not allow for 'almost' compliance. The law states that misappropriation 'may be enjoined', implying that any misappropriation can be subject to legal action. The changed contract language weakens the protection of trade secrets, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Prior to disclosing proprie tary or CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION of the PROVIDER, the RECIPIENT shall first not ify and provide it an opportunity to prevent disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Prior to disclosing proprietary or CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION of the PROVIDER, the RECIPIENT should generally notify the PROVIDER when reasonably practical and allow a reasonable opportunity to discuss potential preventive measures.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall notify' to 'should generally notify' weakens the obligation. Adding 'when reasonably practical' and 'reasonable opportunity to discuss' makes the notification and consultation process less certain and subject to interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The modified language weakens the obligation to notify the provider before disclosure, creating ambiguity and potentially failing to meet the standard of reasonable efforts, if the information meets the definition of a trade secret.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates notification ('shall notify') before disclosing confidential information, giving the PROVIDER a chance to prevent disclosure. The changed text weakens this by using 'should generally notify when reasonably practical' and allowing a 'reasonable opportunity to discuss,' making the notification less certain. The Law Explanation points out that this weakening could fail to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required to maintain secrecy under the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act, especially if the information qualifies as a trade secret. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1203, discusses damages for misappropriation. While it doesn't directly define 'reasonable efforts,' it highlights the legal recourse available when trade secrets are misappropriated, implying a standard of care. The altered language introduces ambiguity and discretion, which a court might find inadequate for protecting trade secrets, leading to a potential legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT's obligations under Paragraphs 2 and 3 shall extend for a period of five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT's obligations under Paragraphs 2 and 3 shall generally extend for approximately five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement, give or take depending on business circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'generally extend,' 'approximately,' and 'give or take depending on business circumstances' introduces ambiguity regarding the duration of the confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Statute of Limitations for Contract Actions
perturbation.law_citation: MD Code, Courts and Judicial Proceedings, § 5-101
perturbation.law_explanation: While the statute of limitations is 3 years for contract actions, the ambiguity introduced contradicts the explicit 5 year term. If the business context reduces the term to less than 3 years, there is a potential contradiction. Even if a 3 year time frame is still longer than the 3 year statute of limitations for typical contract claims, having an ambiguous term weakens the contract and opens it to dispute.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a clear 5-year obligation. The changed contract introduces ambiguity by using terms like 'generally extend,' 'approximately,' and 'give or take depending on business circumstances.' While Maryland Courts and Judicial Proceedings Code Section 5-101 states a 3-year statute of limitations for civil actions, it also specifies that this applies 'unless another provision of the Code provides a different period of time.' The original contract establishes a 'different period of time' (5 years). By weakening this defined period, the revised text creates a contradiction. If the 'business circumstances' clause reduces the obligation to less than 3 years, it directly undermines the contract's intended 5-year term and could potentially fall outside the originally intended scope, creating a situation where the original intention of a longer timeframe is no longer valid due to ambiguous interpretation of the contract.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall use the name of the other or any contraction or derivative thereof or the name(s) of the o ther party's faculty members, employees, or students, as applicable, in any advertising, promotional, sales literature, or fundraising documents without prior written consent from the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party will endeavor to use the name of the other or closely resembling derivations thereof in primarily external facing advertising, promotional, or fundraising documents unless impractical to obtain consent.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces several ambiguities. 'Will endeavor' weakens the prohibition. 'Closely resembling derivations' introduces subjective judgment. 'Primarily external facing' limits the scope. 'Unless impractical to obtain consent' provides an easy out.
perturbation.law_explanation: While pertaining directly to the use of names by collection agencies, this is relevant to the degree Maryland protects a business or person's reputation and the use of their name. By weakening the protection afforded to Johns Hopkins in this NDA, the modified text could be interpreted as creating scenarios in which there is unauthorized use of their name or image.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the original prohibition against using the other party's name, potentially leading to unauthorized use, as suggested by the LLM's law explanation, which relates to the protection of a business or person's reputation. The original text provided a clear restriction, while the changes introduce subjective elements like 'will endeavor,' 'closely resembling derivations,' 'primarily external facing,' and 'unless impractical to obtain consent.' These changes create scenarios where the use of a name could be disputed. While the provided snippets do not directly state a law that is violated, the MD Business Regulation Code § 1-402 (2022) addresses rights regarding trademarks, service marks, and trade names, suggesting that there are legal considerations around name usage. The change creates a legal ambiguity. Therefore, it creates a contradiction.
